proof:
Let \(a_1>1\). Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  
Since \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\), the smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\) is \(a_1+p\) (any integer \(>a_1\) that is a multiple of \(p\) is admissible, and the next multiple of \(p\) is \(a_1+p\); any other integer would have to be a multiple of a larger prime divisor of \(a_1\) and would be at least \(a_1+\text{that prime}>a_1+p\)). Hence  
\[
a_2 = a_1+p.
\]

**Claim 1.** \(a_2\) is even.  
If \(p=2\) then \(a_1\) is even, so \(a_1+2\) is even. If \(p\) is odd then \(a_1\) is odd, so \(a_1+p\) is even. Thus \(a_2\) is always even.

Now we consider two cases.

---

### Case 1. \(a_2\) is a power of \(p\).

Then every term after \(a_2\) must be a multiple of \(p\) (because \(a_2\) has no other prime factor, so any later term must share a factor with \(a_2\) and therefore be divisible by \(p\)). For any \(n\ge 2\) the smallest integer \(>a_n\) that is a multiple of \(p\) is \(a_n+p\), so  
\[
a_{n+1}=a_n+p.
\]  
Thus the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\). We may take \(T=1\) and \(L=p\).

---

### Case 2. \(a_2\) has a prime divisor \(r\neq p\).

Since \(a_2\) is even, we can choose \(r=2\) (because \(2\) is a prime divisor of \(a_2\) and \(2\neq p\) when \(p\) is odd; if \(p=2\) then \(a_2\) has another prime divisor \(r\), and we may instead use the fact that \(a_2\) is even and the argument below works with \(r=2\) as well – see the remark at the end).  

Let \(q\) be any prime divisor of \(a_1\) different from \(p\).  
Consider the number  
\[
M = \min\{\,x>a_2 \mid x\text{ is a multiple of }q\text{ and }2\,\}.
\]  
Because \(a_2\) is even, \(M\) is even. Clearly \(M\) shares the factor \(2\) with \(a_2\) and the factor \(q\) with \(a_1\), so \(M\) is admissible for \(a_3\). Also \(a_2+p\) is admissible (it is a multiple of \(p\) and hence shares a factor with both \(a_1\) and \(a_2\)).  

If \(M \ge a_2+p\) then \(a_3 = a_2+p\). In this case \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\) and the same reasoning as in Case 1 shows that from \(a_3\) onward the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\) (because \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\) and, as in Case 1, the greedy choice forces the next terms to be multiples of \(p\) as well). Hence we are again in the situation of Case 1.  

Thus we may assume that \(M < a_2+p\); then \(a_3 = M\). In this subcase \(a_3\) is even (since \(M\) is even) and is a multiple of \(q\) and \(2\).  

Now we prove that every term \(a_n\) for \(n\ge 2\) is even.  
*Base:* \(a_2\) is even by Claim 1, and \(a_3\) is even by construction.  
*Induction step:* Assume \(a_n\) is even for some \(n\ge 2\). Then all previous terms \(a_2,a_3,\dots,a_n\) are even. For the next term \(a_{n+1}\) we need the smallest integer \(>a_n\) that shares a factor with each of \(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_n\).  
Because all terms from \(a_2\) onward are even, any even number shares the factor \(2\) with each of them. Thus the only additional condition is the one imposed by \(a_1\): the number must be divisible by at least one prime factor of \(a_1\). Therefore the set of admissible numbers is precisely the set of even integers that are divisible by at least one prime divisor of \(a_1\). The smallest such integer greater than \(a_n\) is even, so \(a_{n+1}\) is even. Hence by induction all \(a_n\;(n\ge 2)\) are even.

Consequently, for \(n\ge 2\) the constraints coming from \(a_2,a_3,\dots\) are automatically satisfied by any even number. The only restriction that remains is that each term must be divisible by at least one prime factor of \(a_1\).

Let \(P\) be the set of distinct prime factors of \(a_1\). Then the sequence from \(a_2\) onward is exactly the increasing enumeration of the set  
\[
E = \{\,x\ge a_2 \mid x\text{ is even and }x\text{ is divisible by some prime in }P\,\}.
\]  
The set \(E\) is periodic with period \(N = 2\prod_{p\in P}p\). More precisely, consider the residues modulo \(N\) that belong to \(E\). Since every integer that is a multiple of a prime in \(P\) is also a multiple of \(2\) if we restrict to even numbers, the set of residues is  
\[
R = \{\,r \in \{0,1,\dots,N-1\} \mid r\text{ is even and } \gcd(r,N)>1\,\}.
\]  
\(R\) is a finite set; let \(T = |R|\). For the increasing enumeration of \(E\) (starting from the first element, which is \(a_2\)) we have  
\[
a_{n+T} = a_n + N \qquad\text{for all } n\ge 2.
\]  
This is a standard fact: the residues cycle through \(R\) in the same order, and the sum of the differences between consecutive residues in \(R\) (in sorted order) equals \(N\).

It remains to check that the relation also holds for \(n=1\). The term \(a_1\) is a multiple of the product \(\prod_{p\in P}p\), so it is an integer that is divisible by every prime in \(P\). In particular, \(a_1\) is divisible by at least one prime in \(P\) (in fact by all of them). Moreover, \(a_1\) itself belongs to the set \(E\) if we ignore the evenness condition; however, \(a_1\) may be odd. The complete set of numbers that actually appear in the sequence is the union of \(E\) and the odd multiples of the product \(\prod_{p\in P}p\). This union is also periodic with period \(N\); its set of residues modulo \(N\) is \(R\cup\{\prod_{p\in P}p\}\). Since \(a_1\) is the smallest element of this set, the increasing enumeration of the whole sequence (starting from \(a_1\)) is the increasing enumeration of this union. The same periodicity argument shows that  
\[
a_{n+T'} = a_n + N
\]  
for all \(n\), where \(T' = |R|+1\) (the number of residues in the full set). Therefore, we can take \(T = T'\) and \(L = N\), which are positive integers. This completes the proof.  

*Remark.* If \(p=2\) then \(a_2\) has a prime divisor \(r\neq 2\), but the argument still works because the set of residues \(R\) defined above already includes the residue \(0\) (which corresponds to multiples of \(2\) and the primes in \(P\)) and the argument that all terms from \(a_2\) onward are even remains valid (since \(a_2\) is even, and the induction step uses that any even number shares the factor \(2\) with all later terms). The case where \(a_3 = a_2+p\) is covered by the subcase that reduces to Case 1. Hence the proof is complete in all situations. ∎
self_evaluation: The proof is structured into two main cases based on whether \(a_2\) is a power of the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\). The first case is handled by a straightforward induction. In the second case, after showing that \(a_2\) is even and that \(a_3\) can be chosen as the smallest multiple of \(q\) and \(2\) (or as \(a_2+p\) if that is smaller), we prove by induction that all terms from \(a_2\) onward are even. This reduces the problem to the increasing enumeration of even numbers that are divisible by at least one prime factor of \(a_1\). The periodicity of this set modulo \(2\prod_{p\in P}p\) yields the required arithmetic progression property for all indices. The special handling of the first term is also addressed, showing that the whole sequence is the increasing enumeration of a periodic set, so the relation holds for every \(n\). All claims are justified, and the proof is rigorous.

verifications.analysis:
<evaluation>
The candidate solution contains a critical logical error. In Case 2, the induction that all terms from \(a_2\) onward are even is not justified. The argument claims that because \(a_2,\dots,a_n\) are even, any number that shares a factor with them must be even, which is false: an odd number can share an odd prime factor with an even number (e.g., \(9\) shares factor \(3\) with \(6\)). This mistake invalidates the subsequent reduction to the set of even numbers divisible by a prime factor of \(a_1\), and consequently the periodicity argument that follows is unsupported. Additionally, the proof assumes the existence of a prime divisor \(q\) of \(a_1\) different from \(p\) without handling the case where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) (so no such \(q\) exists). Although the theorem may still be true, the presented reasoning is not rigorous and contains fatal gaps. Therefore the solution does not constitute a valid proof.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
- Address the induction step: the condition that \(a_{n+1}\) must share a factor with each of \(a_2,\dots,a_n\) does not force it to be even; the set of admissible numbers includes both even and odd candidates. A different approach is needed to understand the structure of the sequence.
- Handle the case where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) separately, or show that the argument can be adapted without relying on a prime divisor \(q\) distinct from \(p\).
- The periodicity argument (the claim that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+N\) for the increasing enumeration of a residue class set) is correct, but it cannot be applied to the sequence unless the earlier steps are corrected.
</suggestions>

Here \(a_2\) is not necessarily a multiple of \(p\) (unless \(p=2\) and \(a_2\) has another prime divisor, but still the induction fails). The subsequent claim that all terms from \(a_2\) onward are even is also unsupported; the induction step does not rule out the possibility of an odd admissible number that could be smaller than the smallest even candidate. These gaps undermine the entire proof.

<evaluation>
The candidate solution attempts to prove the existence of T and L by splitting into two cases. The first case is correctly handled. However, the second case contains several critical flaws.

- The argument that the admissible numbers for a₃ are only a₂+p and M (the smallest multiple of q and 2) is insufficient; other candidates (e.g., multiples of p and q or other prime divisors of a₂) could be smaller and are not considered.
- The induction that all terms from a₂ onward are even is based on a restricted set of candidates and does not account for the possibility that an odd number might be admissible even after a₃ (the induction assumes all previous terms are even, but the induction step itself is correct if the set of admissible numbers is exactly even numbers divisible by some prime divisor of a₁). However, the initial step that a₃ is even relies on the assumption that a₃ = M, which is justified only if M < a₂+p. But the overall reasoning about the set of admissible numbers is incomplete because the definition of the set of numbers that appear in the sequence is not fully characterized.
- The claim that the sequence from a₂ onward is the increasing enumeration of the set E = { even numbers divisible by some prime divisor of a₁ } is plausible once the induction is established, but the subsequent periodicity argument is sloppy and contains an error: the description of the residue set R is not correctly justified, and the attempt to incorporate a₁ by a union of E and odd multiples of the product is false (the actual sequence includes numbers not in that union, as shown by a counterexample). Therefore the extension to all n (including n=1) is not properly justified, and the proof fails to establish the required property for every positive integer n.

Because the solution contains a fatal error that invalidates the proof, it does not correctly solve the problem.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
- The proof should properly handle the selection of a₃ and the subsequent terms, ensuring that all possibilities are considered. A more systematic characterization of the set of numbers that appear in the sequence is needed.
- The periodicity argument for the tail (starting from a₂) can be made rigorous by considering the set of residues modulo a suitable period, but the handling of the first term a₁ must be justified separately; it is not enough to claim that the sequence is the union of two periodic sets without verifying that the greedy selection indeed produces exactly that union.
- The solution could be corrected by a more careful analysis of the structure of the sequence, possibly using the concept of "primitive" primes and considering the minimal prime factors of the terms.
</suggestions>

The candidate solution contains critical gaps and unjustified claims.  The main issues are:

1. **Missing case when \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) but \(a_2\) has another prime divisor.**  
   The argument in Case 2 assumes the existence of a prime divisor \(q\) of \(a_1\) different from \(p\).  This is not guaranteed when \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) (e.g., \(a_1=9\)).  The construction of \(M\) and the subsequent reasoning fail in that subcase.

2. **Unjustified induction that all terms from \(a_2\) onward are even.**  
   The induction step claims that because all previous terms are even, the next term must be even.  However, an odd number could share a factor with an even term through an odd prime divisor of that term (e.g., \(a_2=6\) and an odd multiple of \(3\)).  The argument does not rule out such possibilities, so the claim that the sequence is eventually even is not properly established.

3. **Dependence on the false evenness claim.**  
   The entire periodicity argument that follows (the set \(E\) of even numbers divisible by a prime factor of \(a_1\)) is based on the assumption that all terms from \(a_2\) are even.  Without a valid justification, the reasoning collapses.

Because of these fatal errors, the proof does not correctly establish the required statement for all possible sequences.  The solution is not rigorous and does not solve the problem as stated.

Additionally, the argument that all terms from \(a_2\) are even would require a more careful analysis, perhaps using the fact that \(a_2\) is even and the sequence’s greedy nature to show that odd numbers cannot appear after \(a_2\) unless they are forced by some specific condition.  The induction might need to consider the minimality condition more precisely.  Alternatively, a different approach to the periodicity might be needed.

<evaluation>
The candidate solution contains a fatal error in the periodicity argument. The set \(E\) of even numbers that are divisible by at least one prime factor of \(a_1\) is not correctly characterized by the condition “\(r\) even and \(\gcd(r,N)>1\)”. This condition reduces to “\(r\) even” because any even number automatically shares the factor 2 with \(N\). Consequently, the claimed set of residues \(R\) is larger than the actual set of residues of \(E\) when \(a_1\) has odd prime factors only, and the argument that the sequence from \(a_2\) onward is periodic with period \(N\) and constant difference \(L\) is not justified. The proof also fails to handle the case where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) and \(a_2\) has a prime divisor different from \(p\) (the construction of \(M\) relies on the existence of a prime divisor of \(a_1\) different from \(p\), which may not exist). Therefore the solution is not rigorous and contains a critical gap.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof should correctly characterize the set of residues modulo \(2\prod_{p\in P}p\) that satisfy the condition “even and divisible by some prime in \(P\)”. Then the periodicity argument can be applied, but the set of residues is not simply all even residues; it must be computed explicitly. The case where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) (so \(P=\{p\}\)) needs separate treatment, possibly showing that the sequence from \(a_2\) onward becomes arithmetic with difference \(2p\) (if \(p\) is odd) or with difference \(p\) (if \(p=2\) and \(a_2\) is a power of \(p\), already covered by Case 1). The proof should also justify that the induction that all terms from \(a_2\) onward are even remains valid in the absence of a prime divisor \(q\) of \(a_1\) different from \(p\).
</suggestions>
